key_insight: Compatibility of martensitic microstructures across polycrystal grain boundaries is highly constrained, with zero-energy compatibility typically requiring special grain rotations, boundary normals, or higher-order laminates.
engineering_relevance: These bounds help predict when martensitic phase transformations can form low-energy microstructures in polycrystalline alloys, informing microstructure design and shape-memory material performance.
method: Reduced the polycrystal problem to a planar grain-boundary setting.
Analyzed zero-energy configurations for constant gradients and simple laminates in cubic-to-tetragonal and cubic-to-orthorhombic systems.
Used computer-assisted symbolic calculations and quasiconvex-hull bounds to characterize compatibility and the Taylor set.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytical derivations plus computer-assisted symbolic calculation, including measure-zero compatibility sets and new upper bounds on the Taylor set.
watch_item: The strongest compatibility results are specific to certain symmetry classes and zero-energy idealizations, so real materials with defects, kinetics, and nonplanar boundaries may behave differently.
paper_title: Compatibility of Martensitic Microstructures in Polycrystals
